See the worked example: For \(y = x^2\), using the power rule (section 2): \(\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 2x\). At \(x = 3\), gradient \(= 6\). At \(x = 0\), gradient \(= 0\) (minimum on the parabola).
See the worked example: Differentiate \(y = (3x + 1)^5\). Let \(u = 3x + 1\), so \(y = u^5\). \(\dfrac{dy}{du} = 5u^4\), \(\dfrac{du}{dx} = 3\). \(\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 5(3x+1)^4 \times 3 = 15(3x+1)^4\).
See the worked example: Find the tangent to \(y = x^2\) at \((2, 4)\). \(\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 2x\), so at \(x = 2\), gradient \(m = 4\). Tangent: \(y - 4 = 4(x - 2)\), i.e. \(y = 4x - 4\).
See the worked example: Find stationary points of \(y = x^3 - 3x\). \(\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 3x^2 - 3 = 0 \Rightarrow x = \pm 1\). Points: \((-1, 2)\) and \((1, -2)\).

See the worked example: For \(y = x^3 - 3x\), \(\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} = 6x\). At \(x = -1\): \(f''(-1) = -6 < 0\) → local maximum at \((-1, 2)\). At \(x = 1\): \(f''(1) = 6 > 0\) → local minimum at \((1, -2)\).

learn/mathematics/a-level/integration.ht: See the worked example: Given \(\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 3x^2\), find \(y\). \(y = \int 3x^2\,dx = x^3 + C\).
See the worked example: Integrate \(\int (4x^3 - 2x + 5)\,dx\). \(= x^4 - x^2 + 5x + C\).
See the worked example: Evaluate \(\displaystyle\int_1^3 (2x + 1)\,dx\). \(\displaystyle\int (2x+1)\,dx = x^2 + x\). \(\big[x^2 + x\big]_1^3 = (9+3) - (1+1) = 10\).
See the worked example: Area under \(y = x^2\) from \(x = 0\) to \(x = 2\): \(\displaystyle\int_0^2 x^2\,dx = \big[\tfrac{x^3}{3}\big]_0^2 = \tfrac{8}{3}\) square units.

See the worked example: Area between \(y = x\) and \(y = x^2\) from \(x = 0\) to \(x = 1\). On \([0,1]\), \(x \geq x^2\), so upper curve is \(y = x\). \(A = \displaystyle\int_0^1 (x - x^2)\,dx = \big[\tfrac{x^2}{2} - \tfrac{x^3}{3}\big]_0^1 = \tfrac{1}{6}\).

See the worked example: Approximate \(\displaystyle\int_0^2 x^2\,dx\) using 4 strips (\(h = 0.5\)). \(y\)-values: \(0, 0.25, 1, 2.25, 4\). \(\approx \dfrac{0.5}{2}(0 + 2(0.25+1+2.25) + 4) = 2.875\). Exact value: \(\tfrac{8}{3} \approx 2.667\). More strips improve accuracy.
See the worked example: Given \(\displaystyle\int_1^k 2x\,dx = 8\), find \(k\) (\(k > 1\)). \(\big[x^2\big]_1^k = k^2 - 1 = 8 \Rightarrow k^2 = 9 \Rightarrow k = 3\).
learn/mathematics/a-level/limits.html: See the worked example: \(\lim_{x \to 2} (3x + 1) = 7\). As \(x\) approaches 2, \(3x + 1\) approaches 7. Here the limit equals the function value: \(f(2) = 7\).

See the worked example: For \(f(x) = \frac{|x|}{x}\) (sign function for \(x \neq 0\)): \(\lim_{x \to 0^+} \frac{|x|}{x} = \lim_{x \to 0^+} 1 = 1\). \(\lim_{x \to 0^-} \frac{|x|}{x} = \lim_{x \to 0^-} (-1) = -1\). Left and right limits differ, so \(\lim_{x \to 0} f(x)\) does not exist.

See the worked example: For \(f(x) = \begin{cases} x + 1 & x < 2 \\ 5 & x = 2 \\ 7 - x & x > 2 \end{cases}\): Left limit: \(\lim_{x \to 2^-} (x + 1) = 3\). Right limit: \(\lim_{x \to 2^+} (7 - x) = 5\). Limit DNE at \(x = 2\).
See the worked example: Evaluate \(\lim_{x \to 3}\frac{x^2 - 9}{x - 3}\). Factorise: \(\frac{(x-3)(x+3)}{x-3} = x + 3\) for \(x \neq 3\). Limit: \(\lim_{x \to 3}(x + 3) = 6\).
See the worked example: Evaluate \(\lim_{x \to 0}\frac{\sin 3x}{x}\). Rewrite: \(\frac{\sin 3x}{x} = 3 \cdot \frac{\sin 3x}{3x} \to 3 \times 1 = 3\).

learn/mathematics/a-level/linear-transfo: See the worked example: \(T : \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}^2\) defined by \(T(x,y) = (2x + y,\, x - y)\) is linear. Check: \(T(\mathbf{u}+\mathbf{v}) = T(\mathbf{u}) + T(\mathbf{v})\) by direct substitution.

See the worked example: For \(T(x,y) = (2x + y,\, x - y)\): \[ T(\mathbf{e}_1) = T(1,0) = (2,1),\quad T(\mathbf{e}_2) = T(0,1) = (1,-1) \] So \(\mathbf{A} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}\).

See the worked example: Rotation by \(90°\) in \(\mathbb{R}^2\): \[ R = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}, \quad R^2 = \begin{pmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix} \] Two rotations give a half-turn — matrix multiplication encodes composition.

See the worked example: For \(\mathbf{A} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 2 & 4 & 6 \end{pmatrix}\), row 2 is twice row 1. The kernel is \(\{(x,y,z) : x + 2y + 3z = 0\}\), a plane in \(\mathbb{R}^3\), so \(\dim(\ker T) = 2\).

See the worked example: For \(\mathbf{A} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}\), columns are \((1,0,1)^T\) and \((0,0,0)^T\). So \(\text{Im}(T) = \text{span}\{(1,0,1)^T\}\), a line in \(\mathbb{R}^3\), and \(\dim(\text{Im}\, T) = 1\).

See the worked example: For the \(2 \times 3\) matrix above with \(\dim(\ker T) = 2\): \[ \text{rank}(\mathbf{A}) = 3 - 2 = 1 \] Only one column is linearly independent, confirming \(\dim(\text{Im}\, T) = 1\).

See the worked example: Is \(\mathbf{A} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{pmatrix}\) invertible? \(\det(\mathbf{A}) = 1 \cdot 4 - 2 \cdot 3 = -2 \neq 0\), so rank \(= 2 = n\). \(T\) is an isomorphism of \(\mathbb{R}^2\) onto itself.
learn/mathematics/a-level/logarithms.htm: See the worked example: Solve \(3^x = 20\). Take ln: \(\ln(3^x) = \ln 20\) \(x \ln 3 = \ln 20\) \(x = \dfrac{\ln 20}{\ln 3} \approx 2.73\)
See the worked example: Solve \(\log_2(x + 3) = 4\). Exponential form: \(x + 3 = 2^4 = 16\) → \(x = 13\).

See the worked example: Solve \(\ln x + \ln(x − 2) = \ln 3\). \(\ln[x(x−2)] = \ln 3\) → \(x(x−2) = 3\) → \(x^2 − 2x − 3 = 0\) → \((x−3)(x+1) = 0\). Reject \(x = −1\) (not in domain). Solution: \(x = 3\).

learn/mathematics/a-level/matrices.html: See the worked example: Find the inverse of \(\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 5 & 3 \end{pmatrix}\). \(\det = (2)(3) - (1)(5) = 1\). \(A^{-1} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & -1 \\ -5 & 2 \end{pmatrix}\).

See the worked example: Solve \(2x + y = 7\), \(5x + 3y = 16\). \(A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 5 & 3 \end{pmatrix}\), \(B = \begin{pmatrix} 7 \\ 16 \end{pmatrix}\). From section 5: \(A^{-1} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & -1 \\ -5 & 2 \end{pmatrix}\). \(\begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & -1 \\ -5 & 2 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 7 \\ 16 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 5 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}\). Solution: \(x = 5\), \(y = 1\).

See the worked example: \(x = t + 1\), \(y = t^2\). From the first equation: \(t = x - 1\). Substitute: \(y = (x - 1)^2\). This is a parabola.
See the worked example: \(x = t^2\), \(y = t^3\). \(\dfrac{dx}{dt} = 2t\), \(\dfrac{dy}{dt} = 3t^2\). \(\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{3t^2}{2t} = \dfrac{3t}{2}\) (for \(t \neq 0\)).

See the worked example: Find the tangent to \(x = t^2\), \(y = t^3\) at \(t = 2\). Point: \((4, 8)\). Gradient: \(\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{3(2)}{2} = 3\). Tangent: \(y - 8 = 3(x - 4)\), i.e. \(y = 3x - 4\).

See the worked example: Area under \(x = t^2\), \(y = t\) from \(t = 0\) to \(t = 2\). \(A = \displaystyle\int_0^2 t \cdot 2t\,dt = \int_0^2 2t^2\,dt = \big[\tfrac{2t^3}{3}\big]_0^2 = \tfrac{16}{3}\).

learn/mathematics/a-level/trig-functions: See the worked example: State the amplitude and period of \(y = -2\cos(4x)\). Amplitude \(= |-2| = 2\). Period \(= \frac{2\pi}{4} = \frac{\pi}{2}\). The minus sign reflects the graph in the \(x\)-axis.

See the worked example: Describe \(y = 2\sin(3x - \pi) + 1\). Amplitude 2, period \(\frac{2\pi}{3}\), phase shift right by \(\frac{\pi}{3}\), translated up 1 unit. Midline: \(y = 1\). Maximum value: \(1 + 2 = 3\). Minimum value: \(1 - 2 = -1\).

See the worked example: Solve \(\sin x = \frac{1}{2}\) for \(0 \leq x < 2\pi\). Reference angle: \(\frac{\pi}{6}\). Sine positive in QI and QII. Solutions: \(x = \frac{\pi}{6}\) and \(x = \pi - \frac{\pi}{6} = \frac{5\pi}{6}\).

See the worked example: Solve \(2\cos(2x) + 1 = 0\) for \(0 \leq x < 2\pi\). \(\cos(2x) = -\frac{1}{2}\). Let \(u = 2x\), solve \(\cos u = -\frac{1}{2}\) for \(0 \leq u < 4\pi\). \(u = \frac{2\pi}{3}, \frac{4\pi}{3}, \frac{8\pi}{3}, \frac{10\pi}{3}\). So \(x = \frac{\pi}{3}, \frac{2\pi}{3}, \frac{4\pi}{3}, \frac{5\pi}{3}\).

See the worked example: Solve \(2\sin^2 x - \sin x - 1 = 0\) for \(0 \leq x < 2\pi\). Let \(u = \sin x\): \(2u^2 - u - 1 = 0 \Rightarrow (2u + 1)(u - 1) = 0\), so \(u = -\frac{1}{2}\) or \(u = 1\). \(\sin x = 1 \Rightarrow x = \frac{\pi}{2}\). \(\sin x = -\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow x = \frac{7\pi}{6}, \frac{11\pi}{6}\).

learn/mathematics/a-level/unit-circle.ht: See the worked example: Find \(\sin\frac{5\pi}{6}\). \(\frac{5\pi}{6}\) is in quadrant II. Reference angle: \(\alpha = \pi - \frac{5\pi}{6} = \frac{\pi}{6}\). Sine is positive in QII, so \(\sin\frac{5\pi}{6} = \sin\frac{\pi}{6} = \frac{1}{2}\).

See the worked example: Find \(\cos\frac{7\pi}{4}\). QIV, reference angle \(\alpha = 2\pi - \frac{7\pi}{4} = \frac{\pi}{4}\). Cosine is positive in QIV. So \(\cos\frac{7\pi}{4} = \cos\frac{\pi}{4} = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}\).
See the worked example: \(\tan\frac{5\pi}{4}\) is in QIII (tan positive). Reference angle \(\frac{\pi}{4}\). \(\tan\frac{5\pi}{4} = +\tan\frac{\pi}{4} = 1\).

See the worked example: Given \(\sin\theta = \frac{3}{5}\) and \(\theta\) in QII, find \(\cos\theta\). \(\cos^2\theta = 1 - \sin^2\theta = 1 - \frac{9}{25} = \frac{16}{25}\), so \(\cos\theta = \pm\frac{4}{5}\). Cosine is negative in QII, hence \(\cos\theta = -\frac{4}{5}\).

learn/mathematics/undergraduate/converge: See the worked example: \(a_n = \dfrac{3n + 1}{n + 2} \to 3\) as \(n \to \infty\) (divide numerator and denominator by \(n\)).

See the worked example: Test \(\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{1}{n^2 + 1}\). Compare with \(\sum \dfrac{1}{n^2}\) (convergent \(p\)-series, \(p = 2\)). \(\dfrac{a_n}{b_n} = \dfrac{n^2}{n^2+1} \to 1\). Limit comparison → converges.
See the worked example: \(\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{n!}{3^n}\). \(\dfrac{a_{n+1}}{a_n} = \dfrac{(n+1)!/3^{n+1}}{n!/3^n} = \dfrac{n+1}{3} \to \infty\). Ratio test → diverges.
See the worked example: Test \(\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} \dfrac{1}{n\ln n}\). \(\int_2^{\infty} \dfrac{dx}{x\ln x} = \lim_{b\to\infty}[\ln(\ln x)]_2^b = \infty\). Integral diverges → series diverges.
See the worked example: \(\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{(-1)^{n+1}}{n}\) (alternating harmonic). Converges by alternating series test. But \(\sum \dfrac{1}{n}\) diverges → conditionally convergent.

learn/mathematics/undergraduate/differen: See the worked example: Solve \(\dfrac{dy}{dx} = xy\). Separate: \(\dfrac{dy}{y} = x\,dx\). Integrate: \(\ln|y| = \dfrac{x^2}{2} + C\). General solution: \(y = Ae^{x^2/2}\) where \(A = \pm e^C\).

See the worked example: Solve \(\dfrac{dy}{dx} = y^2\), \(y(0) = 1\). \(\dfrac{dy}{y^2} = dx \Rightarrow -\dfrac{1}{y} = x + C\). With \(y(0)=1\): \(C = -1\). Solution: \(y = \dfrac{1}{1-x}\), valid for \(x < 1\) (blows up at \(x = 1\)).
See the worked example: Solve \(y' + 2y = 6e^{-x}\). \(\mu = e^{\int 2\,dx} = e^{2x}\). Then \(\dfrac{d}{dx}(e^{2x}y) = 6e^{x}\). Integrate: \(e^{2x}y = 6e^x + C\), so \(y = 6e^{-x} + Ce^{-2x}\).
See the worked example: Solve \(y'' - 5y' + 6y = 0\). Characteristic: \(r^2 - 5r + 6 = (r-2)(r-3) = 0\), so \(r = 2, 3\). General solution: \(y = C_1 e^{2x} + C_2 e^{3x}\).
See the worked example: Solve \(y'' - 4y' + 4y = 0\). \((r-2)^2 = 0\), so \(r = 2\) (double). General solution: \(y = (C_1 + C_2 x)e^{2x}\).
See the worked example: Solve \(y'' + 4y = 0\). \(r^2 + 4 = 0 \Rightarrow r = \pm 2i\). General solution: \(y = C_1\cos 2x + C_2\sin 2x\).

See the worked example: A tank holds 100 L of brine with 10 kg salt. Fresh water enters at 5 L/min; well-mixed solution leaves at 5 L/min. Let \(S(t)\) = salt mass. \(S' = -\dfrac{5}{100}S = -0.05S\), \(S(0) = 10\). Solution: \(S(t) = 10e^{-0.05t}\) kg.

See the worked example: \(R = 10\,\Omega\), \(L = 0.1\,\text{H}\), \(C = 100\,\mu\text{F}\). \(\dfrac{R}{L} = 100\), \(\dfrac{1}{LC} = 10^5\). Discriminant \(= 10^4 - 4 \times 10^5 < 0\) → underdamped. Natural frequency \(\omega_n = 1/\sqrt{LC} = 316\,\text{rad/s}\); damping ratio \(\zeta = \dfrac{R}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{C}{L}} \approx 0.16\).

See the worked example: \(\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & 5 \\ -1 & -2 \end{bmatrix}\). \(\text{tr} = -4\), \(\det = 4 + 5 = 9\). So \(\lambda^2 + 4\lambda + 9 = 0\). \(\lambda = -2 \pm j\sqrt{5}\). Real part \(-2\) → stable; imaginary part \(\sqrt{5}\) → oscillation at \(\sqrt{5}\,\text{rad/s}\).

See the worked example: \(\mathbf{A} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{pmatrix}\) has \(\lambda = 2, 5\), each with algebraic multiplicity 1. Two distinct eigenvalues in \(\mathbb{R}^2\) \(\Rightarrow\) automatically diagonalisable (geometric mult. \(= 1\) for each, total \(= 2\)).

See the worked example: If \(\mathbf{A}\) has eigenvalues \(1, 3, -2\), then \(\text{tr}(\mathbf{A}) = 1 + 3 + (-2) = 2\) and \(\det(\mathbf{A}) = 1 \cdot 3 \cdot (-2) = -6\). Any matrix similar to \(\mathbf{A}\) has the same trace and determinant.

learn/mathematics/undergraduate/fourier-: See the worked example: \(f(x) = x\) on \((-\pi,\, \pi]\), extended periodically with period \(2\pi\). This produces a sawtooth wave: linear rise, then jump discontinuities at odd multiples of \(\pi\).

See the worked example: Square wave: \(f(x) = 1\) on \((0,\, \pi)\), \(f(x) = -1\) on \((-\pi,\, 0)\). \(a_0 = 0\) (odd average over symmetric interval — actually zero by symmetry). \(a_n = 0\) for all \(n\) (odd function). \(b_n = \dfrac{2}{n\pi}(1 - (-1)^n)\): non-zero only for odd \(n\). So \(f(x) = \dfrac{4}{\pi}\bigl(\sin x + \tfrac{1}{3}\sin 3x + \tfrac{1}{5}\sin 5x + \cdots\bigr)\).

See the worked example: \(f(x) = x\) on \([0,\, \pi]\), odd extension to \([-\pi,\,\pi]\). Sine series only: \(b_n = \dfrac{2}{n}(-1)^{n+1}\). \(f(x) = 2\bigl(\sin x - \tfrac{1}{2}\sin 2x + \tfrac{1}{3}\sin 3x - \cdots\bigr)\) on \([0,\,\pi]\).

See the worked example: For the square wave above, \(\dfrac{1}{\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}1^2\,dx = 2\). Series energy: \(\sum_{k\,\text{odd}} \dfrac{16}{k^2\pi^2} = \dfrac{16}{\pi^2}\bigl(1 + \tfrac{1}{9} + \tfrac{1}{25} + \cdots\bigr) = \dfrac{16}{\pi^2}\cdot\dfrac{\pi^2}{8} = 2\). ✓

learn/mathematics/undergraduate/hyperbol: See the worked example: Simplify \(\cosh^2 x + \sinh^2 x\). Using \(\cosh^2 x - \sinh^2 x = 1\), add \(2\sinh^2 x\) to both sides: \(\cosh^2 x + \sinh^2 x = 1 + 2\sinh^2 x = 2\cosh^2 x - 1\).
See the worked example: Differentiate \(y = \sinh(3x^2)\). By the chain rule: \(\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \cosh(3x^2) \cdot 6x = 6x\cosh(3x^2)\).

See the worked example: Differentiate \(y = \tanh x \cdot \cosh x\). Product rule: \(\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \operatorname{sech}^2 x \cdot \cosh x + \tanh x \cdot \sinh x = \operatorname{sech} x + \sinh^2 x \operatorname{sech} x\).

See the worked example: A cable of length \(L\) between supports at the same height satisfies \(y = a\cosh(x/a)\). The horizontal tension \(T\) and weight per unit length \(w\) are related by \(a = T/w\). Engineers use this to compute sag and maximum stress.

See the worked example: From \(\cos(2x) = 2\cos^2 x - 1\), Osborn's rule gives \(\cosh(2x) = 2\cosh^2 x - 1\). From \(\sin(2x) = 2\sin x \cos x\) (a product of sines), we get \(\sinh(2x) = 2\sinh x \cosh x\) (no sign change needed here since only one sine appears).
learn/mathematics/undergraduate/inverse-: See the worked example: Evaluate \(\displaystyle\int 2x\cos(x^2)\,dx\). Let \(u = x^2\), so \(du = 2x\,dx\). The integrand is \(\cos(u)\,du\). \(= \sin(x^2) + C\).

See the worked example: Evaluate \(\displaystyle\int x\sqrt{x^2 + 1}\,dx\). Let \(u = x^2 + 1\), so \(du = 2x\,dx\) and \(x\,dx = \dfrac{du}{2}\). \(= \dfrac{1}{2}\displaystyle\int u^{1/2}\,du = \dfrac{1}{3}(x^2+1)^{3/2} + C\).
See the worked example: Evaluate \(\displaystyle\int \dfrac{3x^2}{x^3 + 1}\,dx\). Numerator is (up to factor 3) the derivative of \(x^3 + 1\). \(= \ln|x^3 + 1| + C\).

See the worked example: Evaluate \(\displaystyle\int_0^1 \dfrac{2x}{x^2 + 1}\,dx\). Let \(u = x^2 + 1\): limits change from \(x = 0 \to 1\) to \(u = 1 \to 2\). \(= \displaystyle\int_1^2 \dfrac{du}{u} = [\ln u]_1^2 = \ln 2\).

See the worked example: Evaluate \(\displaystyle\int \dfrac{dx}{\sqrt{4 - x^2}}\). Let \(x = 2\sin\theta\), \(dx = 2\cos\theta\,d\theta\). \(= \displaystyle\int d\theta = \theta + C = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2}\right) + C\).

See the worked example: A current \(i(t) = I_0\sin(\omega t)\) flows for half a cycle. Charge transferred: \(Q = \displaystyle\int_0^{\pi/\omega} I_0\sin(\omega t)\,dt\). Let \(u = \omega t\): \(Q = \dfrac{I_0}{\omega}[-\cos u]_0^{\pi} = \dfrac{2I_0}{\omega}\).

learn/mathematics/undergraduate/jordan-n: See the worked example: Consider \[ \mathbf{A} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix} \] Characteristic polynomial: \((\lambda - 2)^2 = 0\), so \(\lambda = 2\) with algebraic multiplicity 2. Eigenvectors: \((\mathbf{A} - 2\mathbf{I})\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}\) gives only \(\mathbf{v} = t(1,0)^T\). Geometric multiplicity \(= 1 < 2\). Not diagonalisable over \(\mathbb{C}\) (or \(\mathbb{R}\)).

See the worked example: For \(\mathbf{A} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix}\), \(\mathbf{v}_1 = (1,0)^T\) is an eigenvector. Solve \((\mathbf{A} - 2\mathbf{I})\mathbf{v}_2 = \mathbf{v}_1\): \[ \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} \Rightarrow y = 1,\; x \text{ free} \] Take \(\mathbf{v}_2 = (0,1)^T\), a generalised eigenvector of rank 2.
See the worked example: \(J_2(2) = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix}\) — exactly the non-diagonalisable matrix from section 2.

learn/mathematics/undergraduate/laplace-: See the worked example: Unit step \(f(t) = 1\) for \(t \geq 0\): \[ F(s) = \int_0^{\infty} e^{-st}\,dt = \frac{1}{s}, \quad s > 0 \]
See the worked example: \(y'' + 4y = 0\), \(y(0) = 1\), \(y'(0) = 0\). \(s^2 Y - s + 4Y = 0 \Rightarrow Y = \dfrac{s}{s^2 + 4}\). \(y(t) = \cos(2t)\).

See the worked example: Find \(\mathcal{L}\{u(t-1)\cdot\sin(t-1)\}\). Let \(f(t) = \sin t\), so \(F(s) = \dfrac{1}{s^2+1}\). By time shift with \(a = 1\): \(\mathcal{L}\{u(t-1)\sin(t-1)\} = e^{-s}\dfrac{1}{s^2+1}\).

learn/mathematics/undergraduate/multivar: See the worked example: For \(f(x,y) = x^2 + y^2\), level curves \(f = c\) are circles centred at the origin. The surface is a paraboloid opening upward.
See the worked example: For \(f(x,y) = x^3 y^2 + 2xy\): \(\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x} = 3x^2 y^2 + 2y\), \quad \(\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial y} = 2x^3 y + 2x\).

See the worked example: For \(f(x,y) = x^2 + y^2\), \(
abla f = (2x, 2y)\). At \((1, 2)\): \(
abla f = (2, 4)\), \(|
abla f| = \sqrt{20} = 2\sqrt{5}\). Steepest ascent direction: unit vector \(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{5}}(1, 2)\).

See the worked example: For \(f(x,y) = xy\) at \((2, 1)\) in direction \(\mathbf{u} = (1/\sqrt{2}, 1/\sqrt{2})\): \(\nabla f = (y, x) = (1, 2)\) at \((2,1)\). \(D_{\mathbf{u}} f = (1, 2) \cdot (1/\sqrt{2}, 1/\sqrt{2}) = 3/\sqrt{2}\).

See the worked example: Let \(z = x^2 y\), \(x = t^2\), \(y = t^3\). Find \(dz/dt\). \(\dfrac{dz}{dt} = 2xy \cdot 2t + x^2 \cdot 3t^2 = 4t^5 + 3t^6\). (Check: \(z = t^7\), so \(dz/dt = 7t^6\) — agrees.)

See the worked example: Classify critical points of \(f(x,y) = x^2 - y^2\). \(\nabla f = (2x, -2y) = (0,0)\) at origin. \(f_{xx} = 2\), \(f_{yy} = -2\), \(f_{xy} = 0\). \(D = 2(-2) - 0 = -4 < 0\) → saddle point at \((0,0)\).

See the worked example: A temperature field \(T(x,y) = 100 - x^2 - 2y^2\) (°C) on a plate. \(
abla T = (-2x, -4y)\). At \((1, 2)\): \(
abla T = (-2, -8)\), so heat flows toward increasing \(x\) and \(y\) (toward cooler regions). Maximum rate of change: \(|
abla T| = \sqrt{68}\) °C/unit.
learn/mathematics/undergraduate/partial-: See the worked example: Classify \(3u_{xx} + 2u_{xy} - u_{yy} = 0\). \(A = 3\), \(B = 2\), \(C = -1\). \(\Delta = 4 - 4(3)(-1) = 16 > 0\) — hyperbolic .

See the worked example: On the rectangle \(0 \leq x \leq a\), \(0 \leq y \leq b\), with \(u(0,y) = u(a,y) = 0\), \(u(x,0) = 0\), \(u(x,b) = f(x)\), separation of variables yields sine-series solutions in \(x\).

See the worked example: \(u_t = k u_{xx}\), \(u(0,t) = u(L,t) = 0\), \(u(x,0) = f(x)\). Let \(u = X(x)T(t)\): \(\dfrac{T'}{kT} = \dfrac{X''}{X} = -\lambda\). With BCs: \(X_n(x) = \sin\dfrac{n\pi x}{L}\), \(\lambda_n = \left(\dfrac{n\pi}{L}\right)^2\). \(T_n(t) = e^{-k\lambda_n t}\). Solution: \(u(x,t) = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} B_n \sin\dfrac{n\pi x}{L}\, e^{-k(n\pi/L)^2 t}\).

learn/mathematics/undergraduate/series.h: See the worked example: For \(a_n = \dfrac{2n + 1}{n}\), find \(\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} a_n\). \(a_n = 2 + \dfrac{1}{n} \to 2\) as \(n \to \infty\).
See the worked example: Evaluate \(\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{5} (3k - 1)\). \(= 3\sum_{k=1}^{5} k - \sum_{k=1}^{5} 1 = 3 \cdot \dfrac{5 \cdot 6}{2} - 5 = 40\).
See the worked example: Sum the first 20 terms of \(5, 8, 11, \ldots\) Here \(a_1 = 5\), \(d = 3\), \(a_{20} = 5 + 19(3) = 62\). \(S_{20} = \dfrac{20}{2}(5 + 62) = 670\).
See the worked example: Sum the first 6 terms of \(3 + 6 + 12 + \cdots\) \(a_1 = 3\), \(r = 2\). \(S_6 = 3 \cdot \dfrac{1 - 2^6}{1 - 2} = 3 \cdot 63 = 189\).
See the worked example: Evaluate \(\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \dfrac{1}{3^k}\). Here \(a = 1\), \(r = \dfrac{1}{3}\). Sum \(= \dfrac{1}{1 - 1/3} = \dfrac{3}{2}\).

See the worked example: A voltage divider network with repeated stages attenuates by factor \(r = 0.5\) each stage. Total signal from an infinite cascade: \(V_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{in}} \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} r^k = \dfrac{V_{\text{in}}}{1 - 0.5} = 2V_{\text{in}}\) (idealised model).

learn/mathematics/undergraduate/sl-probl: See the worked example: For \(y'' + \lambda y = 0\), \(y(0) = y(L) = 0\), non-trivial solutions require \(\lambda_n = (n\pi/L)^2\), \(y_n = \sin(n\pi x/L)\).

See the worked example: Show \(\sin(m\pi x/L)\) and \(\sin(n\pi x/L)\) are orthogonal on \([0,L]\) for \(m \neq n\). Use \(\sin m\theta\sin n\theta = \tfrac{1}{2}[\cos(m-n)\theta - \cos(m+n)\theta]\). Integrating from \(0\) to \(L\) with \(\theta = \pi x/L\) gives zero when \(m \neq n\).

See the worked example: Expand \(f(x) = 1\) on \([0,L]\) in \(\sin(n\pi x/L)\). \(\int_0^L \sin(n\pi x/L)\,dx = \dfrac{L}{n\pi}[1 - (-1)^n]\). \(\int_0^L \sin^2(n\pi x/L)\,dx = L/2\). So \(c_n = \dfrac{2}{n\pi}[1-(-1)^n]\): only odd \(n\) contribute, giving the Fourier sine series of a constant.

learn/mathematics/undergraduate/stokes-t: See the worked example: For a small flat disc of area \(A\) with unit normal \(\mathbf{n}\), centred at a point, Stokes' theorem gives: \[ \oint_{\partial S} \mathbf{F}\cdot d\mathbf{r} \approx (\nabla \times \mathbf{F}) \cdot \mathbf{n}\, A \] so \((\nabla \times \mathbf{F}) \cdot \mathbf{n}\) is circulation per unit area.

See the worked example: Evaluate \(\oint_C (y\,dx - x\,dy)\) where \(C\) is the circle \(x^2 + y^2 = 1\), counter-clockwise. \(P = y\), \(Q = -x\). \(\dfrac{\partial Q}{\partial x} - \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial y} = -1 - 1 = -2\). Integral \(= \iint_D (-2)\,dA = -2\pi\).

learn/mathematics/undergraduate/taylor-s: See the worked example: Maclaurin series for \(f(x) = e^x\). \(f^{(n)}(x) = e^x\), so \(f^{(n)}(0) = 1\) for all \(n\). \(e^x = \displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{x^n}{n!} = 1 + x + \frac{x^2}{2!} + \frac{x^3}{3!} + \cdots\)

See the worked example: Approximate \(\sqrt{1.02}\) using the binomial series. \(\sqrt{1+x} = (1+x)^{1/2} \approx 1 + \dfrac{x}{2} - \dfrac{x^2}{8}\) with \(x = 0.02\). \(= 1 + 0.01 - 0.00005 = 1.00995\) (true value \(\approx 1.00995\)).

See the worked example: Evaluate \(\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0} \frac{e^x - 1 - x}{x^2}\). Using \(e^x = 1 + x + \dfrac{x^2}{2} + \dfrac{x^3}{6} + \cdots\): Numerator \(= \dfrac{x^2}{2} + \dfrac{x^3}{6} + \cdots\), so the limit \(= \dfrac{1}{2}\).

See the worked example: Approximate \(\displaystyle\int_0^{0.5} e^{-x^2}\,dx\). \(e^{-x^2} = 1 - x^2 + \dfrac{x^4}{2} - \cdots\). Integrating term by term: \(\approx \left[x - \dfrac{x^3}{3} + \dfrac{x^5}{10}\right]_0^{0.5} \approx 0.4648\).

See the worked example: The period of a simple pendulum of length \(L\) for small angle \(\theta\) is \(T \approx 2\pi\sqrt{L/g}\), obtained by expanding \(\sin\theta \approx \theta\) in the equation of motion \(\ddot{\theta} + (g/L)\sin\theta = 0\).

See the worked example: Find the radius of convergence of \(\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{x^n}{n}\). \(\left|\dfrac{c_{n+1}}{c_n}\right| = \dfrac{n}{n+1} \to 1\), so \(R = 1\). At \(x = 1\) the series is the harmonic series (diverges); at \(x = -1\) it is alternating (converges). See Convergence for endpoint analysis.
See the worked example: Temperature at a point: \(T = 300\,\text{K}\) Mass: \(m = 5\,\text{kg}\) Trace of a matrix (in Cartesian coordinates): \(\text{tr}(\mathbf{A}) = A_{ii}\)
See the worked example: Under a rotation of coordinates, the scalar \(s = x^2 + y^2\) in 2D remains the same value (it equals \(r^2\) in polar coordinates). Rank-0 tensors need no index subscript.

See the worked example: The displacement vector \(\mathbf{d} = (3, 4)\) has magnitude \(\|\mathbf{d}\| = \sqrt{d_i d_i} = 5\). The magnitude is a rank-0 tensor built from a rank-1 tensor via the dot product (contraction).
See the worked example: For \[ T = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 4 \end{pmatrix}, \quad T_{11} = 2,\; T_{12} = 1,\; T_{21} = 3,\; T_{22} = 4 \] The trace is \(T_{ii} = 2 + 4 = 6\).
See the worked example: Matrix-vector product in index form: \[ y_i = A_{ij} x_j \] For a \(2 \times 2\) matrix, \(y_1 = A_{11}x_1 + A_{12}x_2\) and \(y_2 = A_{21}x_1 + A_{22}x_2\).

See the worked example: If \(\mathbf{T}\) represents stress and the coordinate system rotates by \(Q\), the new components are \(T'_{ij} = Q_{ik} Q_{jl} T_{kl}\). The physical stress state is unchanged; only the numbers in the matrix depend on the axes chosen.

See the worked example: For a uniaxial tension \(\sigma_{11} = 100\,\text{MPa}\) with all other components zero, a face normal to the \(x\)-axis (\(n_1 = 1\)) has traction \(t_1 = 100\,\text{MPa}\). A face normal to \(y\) (\(n_2 = 1\)) has \(t_1 = \sigma_{12} = 0\) — no shear on that face in this case.
learn/mathematics/undergraduate/triple-i: See the worked example: Volume of the unit cube \([0,1]^3\): \(\displaystyle\iiint_E 1\,dV = \int_0^1\int_0^1\int_0^1 dz\,dy\,dx = 1\).

See the worked example: Volume under \(z = 4 - x - y\) over \(0 \leq x \leq 2\), \(0 \leq y \leq 2\): \(V = \displaystyle\int_0^2\int_0^2\int_0^{4-x-y} dz\,dy\,dx = \int_0^2\int_0^2 (4-x-y)\,dy\,dx = \dfrac{16}{3}\).
See the worked example: Volume of a cylinder of radius \(R\), height \(h\): \(V = \displaystyle\int_0^h\int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^R r\,dr\,d\theta\,dz = h \cdot 2\pi \cdot \dfrac{R^2}{2} = \pi R^2 h\).
See the worked example: Volume of a sphere of radius \(R\): \(V = \displaystyle\int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^{\pi}\int_0^R \rho^2\sin\phi\,d\rho\,d\phi\,d\theta = \dfrac{4\pi R^3}{3}\).

See the worked example: For cylindrical coordinates, the Jacobian is: \[ \frac{\partial(x,y,z)}{\partial(r,\theta,z)} = \begin{vmatrix} \cos\theta & -r\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & r\cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{vmatrix} = r \] Hence \(dV = r\,dr\,d\theta\,dz\).

See the worked example: A solid hemisphere of radius \(R\) with uniform density \(\rho_0\). By symmetry, \(\bar{x} = \bar{y} = 0\). Using spherical coordinates: \(\bar{z} = \dfrac{3}{2R^3}\displaystyle\int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^{\pi/2}\int_0^R \rho\cos\phi \cdot \rho^2\sin\phi\,d\rho\,d\phi\,d\theta = \dfrac{3R}{8}\).

See the worked example: Heat stored in a solid with temperature \(T(x,y,z)\) and density \(\rho\): \(Q = \iiint_E c\,\rho\,T\,dV\), where \(c\) is specific heat capacity. Choosing cylindrical coordinates for a pipe simplifies the integral when \(T\) depends only on \(r\) and \(z\).
learn/mathematics/undergraduate/vector-c: See the worked example: For \(f(x,y) = x^2 + xy + y^2\), find \(\nabla f\) at \((1,2)\). \(\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x} = 2x + y = 4\), \(\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial y} = x + 2y = 5\). So \(\nabla f(1,2) = 4\mathbf{i} + 5\mathbf{j}\). The steepest ascent direction at \((1,2)\) is along this vector.
See the worked example: \(\mathbf{F} = x\mathbf{i} + y\mathbf{j} + z\mathbf{k}\) has \(\nabla \cdot \mathbf{F} = 3\) everywhere.

See the worked example: Flux of \(\mathbf{F} = z\mathbf{k}\) through the unit disc \(x^2 + y^2 \leq 1\), \(z = 0\), with upward normal. \(\mathbf{F} \cdot \mathbf{n} = z = 0\) on the surface, so flux \(= 0\).

See the worked example: Show \(\mathbf{F} = 2x\mathbf{i} + 2y\mathbf{j}\) is conservative and find \(\phi\). \(\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = 2x \Rightarrow \phi = x^2 + h(y)\). Then \(\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = h'(y) = 2y\), so \(\phi = x^2 + y^2 + C\). Work from \((0,0)\) to \((3,4)\): \(\phi(3,4) - \phi(0,0) = 25\).

See the worked example: In \(\mathbb{R}^3\), the plane \(W = \{(x,y,z) : x + y + z = 0\}\) is a subspace. Check: if \(\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \in W\), then \((u_1+v_1) + (u_2+v_2) + (u_3+v_3) = 0\), so \(\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{v} \in W\). Similarly for scalar multiples.

See the worked example: Are \((1,2,3)\), \((2,4,6)\) independent in \(\mathbb{R}^3\)? Note \((2,4,6) = 2(1,2,3)\), so \(2\mathbf{v}_1 - \mathbf{v}_2 = \mathbf{0}\) with non-zero coefficients. They are dependent .

See the worked example: Extend \(\{(1,1,0), (0,1,1)\}\) to a basis of \(\mathbb{R}^3\). These two vectors are independent. Add \((0,0,1)\), which is not in their span. The set \(\{(1,1,0), (0,1,1), (0,0,1)\}\) is a basis of \(\mathbb{R}^3\).
